description: Insert needle into stitch from left to right, wrap yarn counterclockwise and pull through.
Insert needle into stitch from right to left, wrap yarn counterclockwise and pull through.
Alternating rows of knit (RS) and purl (WS), producing a smooth fabric on the right side.
Alternating rows of purl (RS) and knit (WS); bumpy texture is the right side.
Insert needle into the stitch one row below the next stitch on the left needle and knit it.
Work the stitch by inserting the needle into the back leg of the loop instead of the front, twisting the stitch.
Work the stitch by inserting the needle into the front leg of the loop (the standard default position).
Slip 2 stitches to cable needle and hold in front, knit 2, then knit 2 from cable needle. Creates a left-leaning cable.
Slip 2 stitches to cable needle and hold in back, knit 2, then knit 2 from cable needle. Creates a right-leaning cable.
Work multiple stitches into one stitch then decrease back to one, creating a three-dimensional bobble on the fabric.
Insert needle as if to knit (from left to right through the front of the stitch).
Insert needle as if to purl (from right to left through the front of the stitch).
Knit into the front of the stitch, then without removing it from the left needle, knit into the back. Single increase, leaves a visible bar.
Purl into the front of the stitch, then purl into the back of the same stitch. Single purl increase.
Lift the horizontal bar between stitches onto the left needle and knit into it. Nearly invisible increase.
Lift the bar between stitches and knit through the back loop to avoid a hole.
Lift the bar between stitches from front to back onto the left needle and knit through the back loop. Left-leaning increase.
Lift the bar between stitches from back to front onto the left needle and knit through the front loop. Right-leaning increase.
Lift the bar between stitches and purl through the back loop. Single purl increase.
Right-leaning purl increase. Lift bar back-to-front and purl through front loop.
Left-leaning purl increase. Lift bar front-to-back and purl through back loop.
Wrap the working yarn around the right needle to create a new stitch and a decorative hole. Foundation of lace knitting.
Vintage term for yarn over (yo). Wrap yarn over the top of the right needle.
Vintage term for yarn over (yo) used between purl stitches. Wrap yarn completely around the needle.
Create a yarn over in the opposite direction, resulting in a twisted stitch on the following row.
Generic instruction to add one or more stitches. Method specified by the pattern.
Insert needle through 2 stitches at once and knit as one. Single right-leaning decrease.
Insert needle through 2 stitches purlwise and purl as one. Single decrease on purl side.
Slip 2 stitches knitwise one at a time, then knit them together through back loops. Single left-leaning decrease.
Slip 2 stitches knitwise, return to left needle, purl together through back loops. Single left-leaning decrease on purl side.
Slip 3 stitches knitwise, knit together through back loops. Double left-leaning decrease.
Slip 3 stitches knitwise, return, purl together through back loops. Double left-leaning decrease on purl side.
Slip 1 knitwise, knit 1, pass slipped stitch over. Single left-leaning decrease.
Slip 1 knitwise, k2tog, pass slipped stitch over. Double left-leaning decrease.
Slip 2 as if to k2tog, knit 1, pass both slipped stitches over. Centered double decrease.
Slip 2 together knitwise, knit 1, pass both slipped stitches over. Symmetrical centered double decrease.
Knit 1, slip this stitch back to left needle, pass second stitch over, return to right needle. Single right-leaning decrease.
Pass the previously slipped stitch over the last worked stitch and off the needle.
Pass 2 slipped stitches over the last worked stitch. Used for double decreases.
Generic instruction to remove one or more stitches. Method specified by the pattern.
Create initial stitches on the needle to begin knitting. Multiple methods exist (long-tail, knitted, cable cast on).
Secure stitches to prevent unraveling when finishing a piece. Also called cast off.
Move a stitch from left needle to right needle without working it.
Slip 1 stitch as if to knit (insert needle from left to right) without working it.
Slip 1 stitch as if to purl (insert needle from right to left) without working it.
Slip a stitch without working it. In knitting used for edgings and joining seams.
Set of short needles with points at both ends, used for knitting in the round on small circumferences.
Short needle used to hold stitches temporarily while working cables.
One complete circuit of stitches when working in the round.
Reference to the left hand or left needle.
Reference to the right hand or right needle.
Short row technique: wrap yarn around next stitch and turn work to prevent holes when working partial rows.
Short row technique that avoids holes by pulling up a double stitch instead of wrapping. Popular modern alternative to w&t.
Hold the working yarn behind the work before the next action.
Bring the working yarn to the front of the work before the next action.
Take the yarn to the back of the work between the needles.
Bring the yarn to the front between the needles. In modern patterns often part of a yarn over.
Alternate abbreviation for yfwd. Bring yarn to front between needles.
Work two or more stitches as one.
Place a stitch marker on the needle at the current position.
Move the stitch marker from the left needle to the right needle without working it.
A stitch marker used to indicate a specific position in the pattern.
The public-facing or outside of the fabric.
The inside or back of the fabric.
Work the bracketed or asterisked section again as many times as directed.
The start of a row, round, or section.
The stitches left over after working a section.
Refers to the next row, round, or instruction.
Carry on working in the established pattern without change.
Every other row or round.
Not an exact measurement; work to roughly the specified dimension.
In the space between two specified points.
The established stitch pattern being worked.
Alternate abbreviation for pattern.
Refers to the row or stitch worked before the current one.
A single loop of yarn on the needle.
A single loop on the needle representing one worked stitch.
The secondary yarn color used in colorwork sections.
The primary or background yarn color in a colorwork project.
Foundation stitch of crochet: yarn over, pull through loop on hook.
Insert hook, pull up loop, yarn over, pull through both loops. Shortest US crochet stitch.
Yarn over, insert hook, pull up loop, yarn over, pull through all 3 loops. Medium height US stitch.
Yarn over, insert hook, pull up loop, (yarn over, pull through 2 loops) twice. Tall US stitch.
Yarn over twice, insert hook, pull up loop, (yarn over, pull through 2 loops) 3 times. US term.
Yarn over 3 times, insert hook, (yarn over, pull through 2 loops) 4 times.
Yarn over 4 times before inserting hook. Extremely tall stitch.
Variation of double crochet with an extra pull-through step, producing a taller looser stitch.
Variation of half double crochet with an extra pull-through step.
Variation of single crochet with an extra pull-through step.
Variation of treble crochet with an extra pull-through step.
Insert hook, yarn over, pull through both the stitch and the loop on hook in one motion.
Yarn over, insert hook around the post from the back. Creates indented ribbed texture.
Yarn over, insert hook around the post from the front. Creates raised ribbed texture.
Insert hook around the post from the back. Subtle texture variation.
Insert hook around the post from the front. Subtle raised texture.
Half double crochet worked around the post from the back.
Half double crochet worked around the post from the front.
Treble crochet worked around the post from the back.
Treble crochet worked around the post from the front.
Double treble worked around the post from the back.
Double treble worked around the post from the front.
Multiple incomplete double crochets worked in one stitch, joined at the top. Protrudes from right side.
Multiple incomplete stitches worked into one stitch or space and joined at the top.
5 complete double crochets in one stitch, then fold and join first to last. Creates a raised bump.
Multiple half-finished half double crochets in one stitch joined at top. Soft puffy texture.
Multiple stitches (usually 5 dc) worked into the same stitch or space, creating a fan shape.
(dc, ch1, dc) worked into the same stitch or space, creating a V shape.
Creates foundation chain and first row of double crochet simultaneously.
Creates foundation chain and first row of single crochet simultaneously.
Basic Tunisian crochet stitch: insert hook, pull up loop (forward pass), then work off loops (return pass).
Insert hook between front and back vertical bars of the stitch below. Creates a fabric resembling knitting.
Bring yarn forward, insert hook as for tss. Creates a purl-like bump on the right side.
Yarn over, insert hook, pull up loop, yarn over, pull through 2 loops, leaving remaining loop on hook.
Insert hook from right to left under the vertical bar. Creates a ribbed effect.
Insert hook under the front vertical bar of the stitch below and pull up a loop.
Yarn over before inserting hook under the vertical bar, producing a medium-height Tunisian stitch.
Yarn over twice, insert hook under the vertical bar, complete as a treble but leave loop on hook.
Insert hook under both vertical bars of the stitch below. Creates a dense fabric.
Insert hook under the vertical bar from right to left (twisted), creating a twisted stitch in Tunisian crochet.
Adds an extra pull-through step to the standard Tunisian simple stitch, creating a slightly looser fabric.
Insert hook under vertical bar, pull up loop and pull directly through loop on hook.
Work 2 stitches into the same stitch to add one stitch.
Work 2 single crochets into the same stitch. Common increase in amigurumi.
Work 2 double crochets into the same stitch.
Insert hook, pull up loop, insert into next stitch, pull up loop, yarn over, pull through all loops.
Work 2 incomplete double crochets, then join at top. Single decrease.
Work 2 incomplete half double crochets and join at top. Single decrease.
Work 2 incomplete trebles and join at top. Single decrease.
Insert hook through front loops only of 2 stitches, pull up loop, yarn over, pull through all. Preferred for amigurumi.
Generic instruction to reduce stitch count. Method specified by the pattern.
Wrap the yarn over the hook before or during a stitch. Fundamental crochet action.
UK/alternative term for yarn over (yo). Wrap yarn over the hook.
Work two or more stitches as one.
The gap created by a chain stitch in the previous row, used as an insertion point.
Adjustable starting ring for working in the round. Creates a closed center with no hole.
Cut yarn, pull tail through last loop and tighten to secure the final stitch.
Chain(s) worked at the start of a row to bring the hook to the correct height for the first stitch.
First half of a Tunisian crochet row: pick up loops across the row.
Second half of a Tunisian crochet row: work off loops from hook.
One complete circuit of stitches when working in the round.
Insert hook under the back loop only of the stitch. Creates a ridged texture.
Insert hook under the front loop only of the stitch. Creates a ridged texture.
The loop of the stitch furthest from you when the right side faces you.
The loop of the stitch closest to you when the right side faces you.
Work through the back loop of the stitch only.
Pass over the next stitch without working into it.
Wet or steam the finished piece to set the stitches and shape the fabric.
Place a stitch marker in the current stitch to mark a specific position.
Move the stitch marker to maintain its position.
Alternate abbreviation for slip marker.
A stitch marker used to indicate a specific position in the pattern.
The public-facing or outside of the fabric.
The inside or back of the fabric.
Work the bracketed or asterisked section again as many times as directed.
The start of a row or round.
The stitches left after working a section.
Refers to the next instruction.
Carry on in the established pattern.
Every other row or stitch.
Not an exact measurement.
In the space between two specified points.
The established stitch pattern being worked.
The row or stitch worked before the current one.
The gap created by chain stitches in the previous row.
A single worked loop.
A single loop of yarn on the hook.
Secondary yarn color used in colorwork.
Primary yarn color in a colorwork project.
Number of stitches and rows per inch/cm. UK term: tension.
Refers to a chain or space previously made, e.g. ch-1 space.
